title: Eclipse: Second Dawn for the Galaxy

walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Galactic Center
walkthrough.text: Welcome to Eclipse, where you are going to explore the galaxy, colonize new star systems, research alien technology, and inevitably blow each other up. Let's get set up. Start by placing the Galactic Center Defense System tile in the middle of the table. This hex tile has the GCDS printed on it, and it is worth four victory points to whoever can conquer it. Leave plenty of room around it because the galaxy is going to expand outward as players explore.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Hex Tiles by Ring
walkthrough.text: Find all the hex tiles and sort them into three separate stacks by the Roman numerals on their backs. Inner ring tiles are marked with a one, middle ring tiles with a two, and outer ring tiles with a three. Shuffle each stack separately and place them face down near the center of the table. Players will draw from these stacks when they explore during the game.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Technology Tiles
walkthrough.text: Lay out all the technology tiles on the tech supply board or in organized rows where everyone can see them. Technologies come in three colors: red for Military, yellow for Grid, and green for Nano. Each tech tile shows its research cost in the upper corner. Make sure the tiles are sorted by color and arranged so that players can easily browse what is available. Technologies are first-come first-served, so keep an eye on what your opponents might want.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Reputation Tile Bag
walkthrough.text: Find all the reputation tiles, which are the small circular tokens with numbers on the back. Drop them all into the cloth bag and give it a good shake. These tiles are drawn as combat rewards and serve as hidden victory points. You draw them face down and peek at them privately. The numbers range from one to four, so there is always a bit of luck in your combat rewards.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Discovery Tiles
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the discovery tiles and place them face down in a supply pile. When players explore new hex tiles, some sectors contain discovery tokens. These give immediate bonuses like free resources, technology, or extra victory points. Keep the pile accessible to everyone.

walkthrough.title: Choose Your Faction
walkthrough.text: Now each player picks a faction. If this is your first game, use the Terran factions, which are the human factions on the simpler side of the faction boards. Experienced players can flip to the alien side for asymmetric powers. Each faction has unique starting technologies, ship blueprints, and special abilities. Take the faction board matching your choice and place it in front of you.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Your Faction Board
walkthrough.text: Your faction board is the heart of your empire. It has three resource tracks along the top: Money, Science, and Materials. Each track is covered with influence discs. The numbers printed beneath the discs show how much of that resource you produce each round. As you place discs onto the map to claim sectors, you uncover higher production values but also increase your upkeep cost. Place all your influence discs on the designated starting positions on your board.

walkthrough.title: Understand the Influence Disc Economy
walkthrough.text: This is the single most important concept in Eclipse, so let's make sure we get it right. Your influence discs do double duty. When you place one on a hex tile, you are claiming that sector and its planets. But that disc came off your faction board, which means the resource track it was covering now shows a higher number. More discs on the map means more production, but it also means higher upkeep costs. If your upkeep exceeds your income, you go bankrupt. We will come back to this in the rules section.

walkthrough.title: Place Your Starting Sector
walkthrough.text: Each player has a home sector tile that matches their faction. Place your home sector tile in a designated starting position around the galactic center. The rulebook shows the correct positions based on player count. Your starting sector already has planets that produce resources. Place one influence disc from your faction board onto your home sector to show you control it.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Your Ship Blueprints
walkthrough.text: Each faction starts with four ship blueprint tiles: Interceptor, Cruiser, Dreadnought, and Starbase. Place these next to your faction board. Each blueprint has slots for ship components like weapons, drives, computers, shields, hulls, and energy sources. Your starting blueprints come pre-loaded with basic components. During the game, you will research new technologies and upgrade these blueprints to build better ships.

walkthrough.title: Place Your Starting Ships
walkthrough.text: The exact starting ships depend on your faction, but most factions begin with one Interceptor. Take the matching ship miniature from your supply and place it on your home sector hex. These plastic miniatures look fantastic on the table. Keep your remaining ship miniatures nearby because you will be building more as the game progresses.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Colony Ships and Population Cubes
walkthrough.text: Each player receives population cubes in their color. These small cubes represent your colonists and are placed on planet slots within sectors you control. Your starting sector already has some planets. Place population cubes on the appropriate planet slots of your home sector as shown on your faction board. The type of planet determines which resource it produces: orange for Money, pink for Science, and brown for Materials.

walkthrough.title: Set Out Ambassador Tiles
walkthrough.text: Place the ambassador tiles in a shared supply area. These are used when players form diplomatic relations. Each player has ambassador tiles in their color. When two players agree to a diplomatic pact, they exchange ambassador tiles. Ambassadors are worth one victory point each at the end of the game, so diplomacy has tangible rewards.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Turn Order Track
walkthrough.text: Set up the turn order track and place each player's marker on it. In the first round, turn order is determined by faction. In subsequent rounds, the player who passed first in the previous round goes first. This is a crucial strategic element. Passing early gives you a better position next round, but it means you take fewer actions this round.

walkthrough.title: Place the Round Tracker
walkthrough.text: Put the round marker on the first space of the round track. Eclipse is played over exactly nine rounds. Each round follows the same structure: players take actions in turn order, then everyone resolves combat, then everyone collects resources and pays upkeep. Nine rounds might not sound like a lot, but every round is packed with decisions.

walkthrough.title: Verify Your Starting Resources
walkthrough.text: Before we begin, let's double-check that everyone has their starting resources correct. Check your faction board for your initial Money, Science, and Materials stockpile. Most Terran factions start with two of each resource in their storage. Make sure your influence discs are on the correct starting positions and your starting ships and population cubes are placed. Ready to conquer the galaxy? Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: Round Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: Eclipse is played over nine rounds, and every round follows the same three-phase structure. First comes the Action Phase, where players take turns choosing actions. Second is the Combat Phase, where battles are resolved in every sector that has ships from multiple players. Third is the Upkeep Phase, where you collect resources, pay your upkeep costs, and check for bankruptcy. Let's dive into each phase.

walkthrough.title: The Action Phase — Taking Turns
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you either perform one action or pass. If you take an action, you place one of your influence discs from your faction board onto the action track to mark it. This is important because every action you take costs you one more influence disc, which increases your upkeep. You can take as many turns as you want before passing, but the more actions you take, the more expensive your round becomes.

walkthrough.title: The Seven Actions
walkthrough.text: You have seven possible actions to choose from. Explore lets you discover new sectors. Influence lets you claim or abandon sectors. Research lets you buy new technology tiles. Upgrade lets you install components on your ship blueprints. Build lets you construct new ships and structures. Move lets you send your ships across the galaxy. And Diplomacy lets you form or break alliances. Each action can only be taken once per turn, but you can repeat the same action type on different turns within the same round.

walkthrough.title: Action: Explore
walkthrough.text: When you explore, you draw hex tiles from one of the three ring stacks. The inner ring has the richest sectors but is closest to the dangerous Galactic Center. The outer ring is safer but has fewer resources. Draw one or two tiles depending on your faction abilities, then choose one to place adjacent to a sector you already control. The new tile must connect via wormhole connections on the tile edges. If you like the tile, place an influence disc on it to claim it. If you do not want it, discard it face down.

walkthrough.title: Action: Influence
walkthrough.text: You can place a new disc from your faction board onto a sector you have ships in to claim it, or you can pick up a disc from a sector you want to abandon. Abandoning sectors is sometimes necessary to avoid bankruptcy. You get two influence moves when you take this action. This is also how you colonize planets within a sector, by placing population cubes onto empty planet slots.

walkthrough.title: Action: Research
walkthrough.text: When you research, you buy one technology tile from the supply board and place it on your faction board. You pay the research cost in Science resources, minus any discounts from technologies you already own. Each row of tech on your faction board gives a cumulative discount, so the more tech you research in a single color, the cheaper future techs of that color become. Technologies provide ship components, special abilities, or victory points. This is one of the most powerful actions in the game.

walkthrough.title: Action: Upgrade
walkthrough.text: You get two upgrade moves per action. Pick a ship type, remove an old component, and install a new one that you have researched. The upgrade applies to the blueprint, which means all future ships of that type are built with the new design. Existing ships on the map are not affected until they are rebuilt. This is how you customize your fleet to counter your opponents' strategies.

walkthrough.title: Ship Energy Management
walkthrough.text: Every component on a ship requires energy to function. Your blueprint has a power track that shows your total energy supply from energy source components. Weapons, drives, computers, shields, and hulls all consume energy. If you install more components than your energy can support, the ship design is illegal and you cannot build it. Always check your energy balance when upgrading. This constraint is what keeps ship design interesting: you cannot have everything, so you have to make tradeoffs.

walkthrough.title: Action: Build
walkthrough.text: When you build, you spend Materials to construct ships and starbases. You can build up to two ships or starbases per Build action. Ships are placed in sectors you control. Interceptors are cheap and fast. Cruisers are the workhorses. Dreadnoughts are expensive powerhouses. Starbases are immobile defensive platforms that do not move but can be very tough. Check the cost on your blueprint tiles. You are limited by both your Materials and your total ship supply.

walkthrough.title: Action: Move
walkthrough.text: You get three move sub-actions. For each sub-action, pick one ship and move it a number of sectors up to its drive value. Ships move through wormhole connections between adjacent hexes. You can move the same ship multiple times using different sub-actions to cover more ground. Ships cannot pass through sectors controlled by other players unless there is a diplomatic agreement. Moving into a sector with enemy ships triggers combat at the end of the round.

walkthrough.title: Action: Diplomacy
walkthrough.text: Diplomacy lets you propose an alliance with another player. If both players agree, they exchange ambassador tiles. Ambassadors are worth one victory point each at game end. While you have a diplomatic relation, your ships can pass through each other's sectors without triggering combat. However, if you later attack a player you have a diplomatic agreement with, you must return their ambassador tile and take the Traitor Card, which is worth negative two victory points. Betrayal has consequences.

walkthrough.title: Passing and Turn Order
walkthrough.text: Instead of taking an action, you can pass. Once you pass, you are done for the round. You cannot take any more actions. The first player to pass gets the best position on the turn order track for the next round. The second player to pass gets the second position, and so on. This creates a fascinating tension: do you take one more action to strengthen your position, or do you pass early to get a better turn order next round? Taking too many actions also means higher upkeep costs, so there is a real penalty for going long.

walkthrough.title: Combat Phase — Engagement
walkthrough.text: After all players have passed, combat happens in every sector where ships from two or more players coexist. Combat is resolved one sector at a time. If there are multiple combats, the active player in turn order chooses the order. Each combat continues in rounds until only one player's ships remain or both sides retreat.

walkthrough.title: Combat Phase — Initiative and Dice
walkthrough.text: Interceptors typically have the best initiative, firing first. Each ship rolls a number of attack dice equal to its weapons. The dice are six-sided, and you need to roll a six or higher to hit. Wait, six or higher on a six-sided die? That is where computers come in. Your computer value is added to every die roll. So if you have a plus two computer, a roll of four becomes six, which is a hit. Shields work the opposite way, subtracting from the attacker's roll.

walkthrough.title: Combat Phase — Damage and Destruction
walkthrough.text: Each hit deals one damage. Your ship's hull value determines how many hits it can absorb. If a ship takes more hits than its hull value, it is destroyed and removed from the board. Interceptors typically have one hull point, so one hit destroys them. Dreadnoughts can have three or more hull points and take a beating. Destroyed ships go back to your supply and can be rebuilt later. The attacker distributes hits among enemy ships however they choose.

walkthrough.title: Combat Phase — Retreat and Reputation
walkthrough.text: If you retreat, your ships are flipped face down and cannot fight again this round. When combat ends with a clear winner, the winning player draws reputation tiles from the bag. You draw one tile per enemy ship type destroyed, so destroying a mix of Interceptors, Cruisers, and Dreadnoughts earns more draws. You peek at the tiles and keep the best one, returning the rest to the bag.

walkthrough.title: Upkeep Phase — Production
walkthrough.text: After all combats are resolved, the Upkeep Phase begins. First, every player collects resources. Look at your faction board and check which numbers are now uncovered on each resource track. The highest uncovered number on your Money track is how much Money you produce. Same for Science and Materials. Remember, each influence disc you removed from the board uncovered a higher number, so more expansion means more production.

walkthrough.title: Upkeep Phase — Paying Costs
walkthrough.text: Count the total number of influence discs you have placed on the action track and the map. This number determines your upkeep cost, which increases on a steep curve. A few discs cost almost nothing, but spreading yourself too thin can cost ten or more Money per round. Pay your upkeep in Money. If you cannot pay, you must remove influence discs from the map until your upkeep drops to an affordable level. Losing sectors is devastating, so manage your economy carefully.

walkthrough.title: Upkeep Phase — Cleanup
walkthrough.text: After paying upkeep, return all influence discs from the action track back to your faction board. This resets your production tracks and prepares you for the next round. Move the round marker forward one space. If it was round nine, the game is over. Otherwise, start the next round with the new turn order.

walkthrough.title: Ancients and the GCDS
walkthrough.text: Some hex tiles contain Ancient ships when first explored. These powerful NPC ships guard valuable sectors and must be defeated in combat before you can claim the sector. The Galactic Center Defense System is the most powerful Ancient, guarding the center tile. Defeating the GCDS is worth four victory points and is a major accomplishment. Ancient ships do not move or take actions, but they fight back during combat using their printed stats.

walkthrough.title: Discovery Tiles
walkthrough.text: When you explore a sector with a discovery symbol, place a discovery tile face down on it. When you colonize that sector by placing a population cube on a planet, you flip the discovery tile and claim its benefit. Benefits include free resources, technologies, ship components, or victory points. You can choose to keep the tile face up for its ongoing benefit or flip it for an immediate two victory points. Discoveries add a nice element of surprise to exploration.

walkthrough.title: Technology Tracks and Discounts
walkthrough.text: Your faction board has three rows for placing researched technologies, one for each color. As you fill a row, you unlock cumulative research discounts for that color. Your first Military tech costs full price, but your second is cheaper, your third cheaper still, and so on. This creates specialization incentives. Going deep in one tech color makes future research in that color very affordable. Some technologies also advance your faction on the VP track.

walkthrough.title: Game End and Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends after round nine. Add up your victory points from six sources. First, count the VP value printed on each sector tile you control. Second, reveal your reputation tiles and add their values. Third, count one point for each ambassador tile you hold. Fourth, add VP from your technology tracks. Fifth, add any VP from discovery tiles you kept. Sixth, subtract two points if you hold the Traitor card. Highest total wins. Ties are broken by the player with the most resources remaining.

Action Phase: take turns choosing actions or pass
Combat Phase: resolve all battles
Upkeep Phase: produce resources, pay costs, cleanup

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Galaxy
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first two rounds with three example players: the Terran Directorate, the Terran Federation, and the Terran Alliance. Each player will demonstrate different actions and strategies. We will also watch one player make some common beginner mistakes so you can learn what to avoid. Ready to explore the stars? Let's go.

walkthrough.title: Round 1 Begins
walkthrough.text: Everyone has their faction boards set up with influence discs covering the resource tracks, starting ships in home sectors, and population cubes on their starting planets. The round marker is on space one. Each player has a stockpile of starting resources. Remember, every action you take costs an influence disc, and that disc increases your upkeep. Let's see how the first round unfolds.

walkthrough.title: Directorate: Explore
walkthrough.text: They draw two tiles from the outer ring stack and examine them. One has two planets and a discovery token. The other has one planet and no special features. They pick the two-planet tile and place it adjacent to their home sector, connecting the wormholes. Then they place an influence disc from their faction board onto the new tile to claim it. Great start: two new planets to colonize means more resource production soon.

walkthrough.title: Why Exploring Early Matters
walkthrough.text: Exploring in the first round is almost always the right move. The best sectors get claimed first, and the outer ring tiles are relatively safe since opponents are far away. Early exploration gives you planets to colonize, which means more resources in later rounds. If you wait too long, the good tiles will be taken and you will be boxed in with no room to grow.

walkthrough.title: Federation: Explore
walkthrough.text: The Federation also explores, drawing from the outer ring. They find a tile with one money planet and one science planet. They place it adjacent to their home sector and claim it with an influence disc. Two factions expanding outward, building their empires one hex at a time. Notice how both players chose Explore as their first action. This is typical of experienced play.

walkthrough.title: Alliance: The First Mistake — Building Before Upgrading
walkthrough.text: The Alliance player is excited about their fleet and wants ships right away. They choose the Build action and spend Materials to construct two Interceptors with the default blueprint. But the default Interceptor has weak weapons, no computer bonus, and minimal hull. These ships are essentially target practice for anyone who upgrades their blueprints first. The Alliance has spent precious Materials and an action on ships that will be outclassed within a round or two.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Upgrade Before You Build
walkthrough.text: Building ships with default blueprints is a classic beginner trap. Your starting ship designs are bare-bones. Before committing Materials to a fleet, research useful technologies and upgrade your blueprints. A single Interceptor with improved weapons and a computer is worth more than three stock Interceptors. Always ask yourself: is my blueprint good enough to justify building right now?

walkthrough.title: Directorate: Influence to Colonize
walkthrough.text: Back to the Directorate. They take the Influence action and use their two moves to place population cubes on the planets in their newly explored sector. They put one cube on an orange planet for Money production and one on a pink planet for Science production. Now those planets are generating resources for the Directorate starting next Upkeep Phase. This is how you grow your economy.

walkthrough.title: Federation: Research
walkthrough.text: The Federation decides to invest in technology early. They take the Research action and buy Improved Hull from the Military tech row, paying the Science cost. This component can now be installed on any of their ship blueprints. Smart play: getting tech early means you can upgrade your ships before committing Materials to building them.

walkthrough.title: Alliance: The Second Mistake — Overexpanding
walkthrough.text: The Alliance explores and claims another sector. Now they have their home sector plus two explored sectors, each with an influence disc. That is three discs on the map. They then explore and claim a fourth sector on their next turn. Four sectors already, and it is only round one. But look at the upkeep track on their faction board: four discs on the map plus the discs on the action track from all these actions means their upkeep cost is climbing fast. When the Upkeep Phase hits, the Alliance is going to be in trouble.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Watch Your Upkeep
walkthrough.text: Overexpanding is the most common way new players lose Eclipse. Every sector you claim costs an influence disc, and every action you take costs another disc on the action track. The upkeep curve is not linear. It gets steeper as you place more discs. Three or four discs is manageable. Six or seven can bankrupt you. Before claiming a new sector, always check your upkeep track and make sure your Money production can cover the cost. It is better to have three well-developed sectors than five empty ones you cannot afford.

walkthrough.title: Directorate: Research
walkthrough.text: This gives them a powerful weapon component for their ship blueprints. Notice that the Directorate has been methodical: explore first, colonize second, research third. They are building a solid foundation before spending on ships.

walkthrough.title: Passing Decisions
walkthrough.text: The Directorate decides to pass after three actions this round. They want a good turn order position for round two, and their upkeep is still manageable. The Federation takes one more action then passes. The Alliance, having taken five actions already, is the last to pass. This means the Alliance will go last in round two, which is a disadvantage when claiming newly explored sectors.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Know When to Pass
walkthrough.text: Passing is an action in itself. The earlier you pass, the better your turn order next round. But passing early means fewer actions this round. The sweet spot is usually three to four actions per round in the early game. Taking five or six actions in round one is almost always too many because it floods the action track with discs, inflates your upkeep, and gives you the worst turn order for round two. Quality over quantity.

walkthrough.title: Upkeep Phase — The Alliance's Reckoning
walkthrough.text: The Upkeep Phase arrives and all three players collect resources based on their uncovered track values. The Directorate and Federation are in good shape: they produce enough Money to cover their modest upkeep. But the Alliance has a problem. Their upkeep cost from all those influence discs is higher than their Money production. They are bankrupt. They must remove influence discs from the map, abandoning sectors until their upkeep drops below their income. The Alliance loses a sector they spent an action claiming. Those Materials spent on stock Interceptors feel even worse now.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: The Economy Is Everything
walkthrough.text: Eclipse is a game about managing your influence disc economy. Every decision connects back to it. Can you afford to hold this sector? Can you afford another action this round? Should you abandon a marginal sector to free up a disc? Players who ignore their resource tracks and just build and fight will lose to players who carefully balance expansion with income. Check your upkeep cost before every action.

walkthrough.title: Round 2 — Directorate: Upgrade
walkthrough.text: They take the Upgrade action and install Neutron Bombs onto their Interceptor blueprint, replacing the basic Ion Cannon. They also add an energy source to power the new weapon. Now every Interceptor they build will be significantly more dangerous than the stock design. Two upgrade moves, one action. Efficient.

walkthrough.title: Federation: Build
walkthrough.text: They construct two Cruisers, placing them in their home sector. These Cruisers have the upgraded hull, making them tougher than stock. The Federation is preparing for potential conflict in the mid-game. Building after researching and upgrading is the correct sequence.

walkthrough.title: Alliance: The Third Mistake — Ignoring the Economy
walkthrough.text: The Alliance, now going last due to their late pass, takes the Explore action again. They find a tile in the middle ring with three planets. It looks amazing, so they claim it. But they have not colonized most of their existing planets yet. Those sectors are claimed but producing nothing. The Alliance has spread wide but not deep. Three sectors with uncolonized planets are worse than two sectors with every planet producing resources.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Develop What You Have Before Grabbing More
walkthrough.text: New players often rush to explore and claim as many sectors as possible. But claiming a sector without colonizing its planets is a net negative: you pay upkeep for the influence disc but get no resource production from the empty planets. Before exploring another sector, make sure you have used the Influence action to place population cubes on your existing planets. Develop before you expand.

walkthrough.title: Alliance: Recovery
walkthrough.text: This turn, the Alliance takes the Influence action and places population cubes on two planets in sectors they already control. Now those planets are producing Money and Materials. On their next turn, they should consider abandoning their least valuable sector to reduce upkeep. It stings to give up territory, but fiscal responsibility wins games. The Alliance is starting to stabilize.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Preview
walkthrough.text: By round three or four, the galaxy is filling in. Players' borders are bumping up against each other. Diplomatic offers start flying. Military technology becomes more tempting. The GCDS in the center is calling to anyone brave enough to challenge it. The exploratory early game transitions into a tense mid-game of positioning, alliances, and arms races. This is where Eclipse really comes alive.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen all the major actions in practice, watched the Alliance stumble through three classic beginner mistakes, and learned the importance of balancing expansion with economy. Remember: explore early, upgrade before building, watch your upkeep, colonize your planets, and pass at the right time. You are ready to conquer the galaxy.

walkthrough.title: Before You Launch
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full nine-round game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and economy management advice.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to technologies, ship components, and game terms.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

Covers first 2 rounds with all major actions demonstrated
Alliance makes common beginner mistakes for learning

4 discs on map + action track discs = massive upkeep

bullets: Overexpanding is the #1 new player mistake
Upkeep curve is non-linear — 6-7 discs can bankrupt you
Always check upkeep track before claiming a new sector
3 developed sectors > 5 empty unaffordable sectors

bullets: Earlier pass = better turn order next round

5-6 actions in round 1 = inflated upkeep + worst turn order
Quality of actions > quantity of actions

Must remove discs from map, losing claimed sectors
Directorate and Federation fine with modest upkeep

Can you afford this sector? This action? This round?
Balance expansion with income production
Check upkeep cost before EVERY action

Adds energy source to power the new weapon

Existing planets still uncolonized — no production

summary.title: Before You Launch
bullets: Check out General Tips → economy management and strategy basics
Reference the Appendix → technologies, components, and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, here are the mistakes that sink new Eclipse players. First, overexpanding by claiming too many sectors too fast and going bankrupt from upkeep. Second, building ships before upgrading your blueprints, wasting Materials on ships that get destroyed immediately. Third, ignoring your resource tracks and not colonizing planets, so your sectors produce nothing. Fourth, taking too many actions per round and ending up last in turn order with massive upkeep. Fifth, neglecting exploration in the early rounds and getting boxed in with nowhere to grow. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the biggest traps.

walkthrough.title: The Influence Disc Economy
walkthrough.text: Eclipse's entire strategy revolves around your influence discs. Every disc you place on the map or action track increases your upkeep. Think of each disc as a subscription fee: you pay for it every single round. Before you claim a new sector, calculate whether the resource production from its planets will pay for the upkeep increase. A sector with no useful planets or one that you cannot colonize immediately might actually cost you more than it earns. The best players always know exactly how many discs they can afford to deploy.

walkthrough.title: Research Timing
walkthrough.text: Research is the most powerful action in the game when timed correctly. In the early game, focus on technologies that enable your strategy. If you plan to fight, grab weapon and computer upgrades early. If you plan to build an economic empire, look at technologies that improve production or reduce costs. The cumulative discount system rewards specializing in one tech color over spreading across all three. Going deep in Military tech means your fourth and fifth Military techs are nearly free. Plan your tech path before the game starts if you can.

walkthrough.title: Ship Design Philosophy
walkthrough.text: Ship design is one of Eclipse's most satisfying systems, but new players often make it too complicated. Here is a simple framework. Interceptors should be fast and cheap with decent initiative. Their job is to fire first and screen for your heavier ships. Cruisers are your flexible middle ground: give them a solid weapon and a computer. Dreadnoughts are your heavy hitters: load them with weapons, computers, and hull to make them devastating. Starbases are your anchors: stack them with shields and hull to hold key sectors. Always check your energy balance before finalizing a design.

walkthrough.title: When to Fight and When to Negotiate
walkthrough.text: Combat in Eclipse is expensive. You lose ships, you spend actions moving and rebuilding, and the aftermath can leave you vulnerable. Before you pick a fight, ask yourself three questions. One: can I actually win this battle with my current fleet? Two: is the sector I am fighting over worth the cost of the war? Three: will this fight leave me exposed to a third player who swoops in while I am weakened? Sometimes the best move is diplomacy. An ambassador tile is a guaranteed one victory point with no risk. The Traitor Card is negative two points, so betrayal needs to earn you at least three points to break even.

walkthrough.title: Passing Strategy
walkthrough.text: Passing is not giving up. Passing is a strategic tool. The earlier you pass, the better your turn order next round, and the fewer discs you have on your action track driving up upkeep. In the early game, three to four actions per round is a healthy target. In the mid-game, you might push to five if you are in a strong economic position. Late game, you may want to pass early and let opponents burn actions while you conserve resources. Watch what your opponents are doing: if they have all passed and you are still going, you might be overextending.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Awareness
walkthrough.text: Eclipse scores from multiple sources, and the best players keep all of them in mind throughout the game. Sectors provide points based on their ring: inner ring tiles are worth more than outer ring. Reputation tiles from combat can be worth one to four points each. Ambassador tiles from diplomacy are one point each. Technology tracks can earn you victory points as you advance. Discovery tiles offer two points if flipped. And the GCDS is worth four points but requires a serious fleet to conquer. Diversify your scoring sources rather than going all-in on one strategy.

walkthrough.title: Round-by-Round Pacing
walkthrough.text: Eclipse has nine rounds, and your priorities should shift as the game progresses. In rounds one through three, focus on exploration, colonization, and research. Build your economy and your tech base. In rounds four through six, start building your fleet, upgrading blueprints, and positioning for conflicts or diplomacy. In rounds seven through nine, it is scoring time. Make decisive moves: conquer key sectors, fight for the GCDS, or lock in diplomatic VP. Every action in the final rounds should directly contribute to your final score. Players who are still exploring in round seven are usually too late.

bullets: Overexpanding: too many influence discs deployed = bankruptcy
Building ships before upgrading blueprints = wasted Materials
Ignoring resource tracks — uncolonized planets produce nothing
Too many actions per round = high upkeep + worst turn order
Not exploring early = boxed in with nowhere to grow

Always know exactly how many discs you can afford

Starbases: anchor defense — shields + hull to hold key sectors
Always verify energy balance before finalizing designs

3 questions: Can I win? Is the sector worth it? Will a 3rd player exploit me?
Ambassador = 1 VP guaranteed, no risk
Traitor Card = -2 VP, betrayal must net 3+ VP to break even

bullets: Early pass = better turn order + fewer upkeep discs

If all opponents passed and you're still going, you're overextending
Passing is a strategic tool, not giving up

summary.title: Scoring Awareness

Reputation tiles: 1-4 VP from combat

summary.title: Round-by-Round Pacing
bullets: Rounds 1-3: explore, colonize, research — build economy/tech base
Rounds 4-6: build fleet, upgrade blueprints, position for conflict/diplomacy
Rounds 7-9: scoring sprint — every action must contribute to final score
Still exploring in round 7 = too late

walkthrough.title: Economic Engine Strategy
walkthrough.text: The economic engine approach focuses on maximizing resource production through aggressive colonization and careful upkeep management. The core idea is to explore sectors with multiple planets, colonize them immediately, and use the resulting resource surplus to fund everything else. Prioritize sectors with three planets, especially those mixing Money and Science. Research Grid technologies that improve production or reduce costs. The economic player may not have the biggest fleet, but they can afford to research and build exactly what they need when they need it.

walkthrough.title: Building the Economic Engine
walkthrough.text: Start by exploring two sectors in rounds one and two, choosing tiles with the most planets. Use the Influence action aggressively to colonize every planet as fast as possible. Research Grid technologies for production bonuses and cost reduction. By round three, your economy should be producing significantly more than your opponents. The key discipline is never claiming a sector you cannot colonize within one round. Empty sectors drain your economy. A lean, fully-developed empire of four sectors can out-produce a sprawling empire of seven half-empty ones.

walkthrough.title: Military Dominance Strategy
walkthrough.text: The military dominance approach focuses on building the strongest fleet in the galaxy and taking what you want by force. Research Military technologies early for superior weapons, computers, and hull components. Upgrade your Dreadnought blueprint into a monster that can take multiple hits and dish out devastating damage. The goal is to make your fleet so intimidating that opponents either avoid you entirely or lose badly when they fight. Combat victories earn reputation tiles, which are hidden VP that can swing the final score.

walkthrough.title: Executing the Military Strategy
walkthrough.text: In rounds one and two, explore minimally and focus on Research and Upgrade actions. Get Plasma Missiles or Antimatter Cannons onto your Dreadnought blueprint along with computers for accuracy. By round three, build two or three Dreadnoughts and start projecting power. Target opponents' border sectors, especially those with high VP values. The reputation tiles you earn from combat are often worth more than the sectors themselves. Aim to destroy a variety of ship types for maximum tile draws. Keep enough economy to sustain your war machine, but do not over-invest in planets you will not need.

walkthrough.title: GCDS Rush Strategy
walkthrough.text: Conquering the Galactic Center Defense System is worth four victory points, and it is one of the highest single-point plays in the game. The GCDS is a fearsome Ancient with high stats, so you need a purpose-built fleet to take it down. Research technologies that give you high-damage weapons and strong hull values. Build three or four upgraded Cruisers or Dreadnoughts and push toward the center through the inner ring. The key timing is to strike in rounds five or six when your fleet is strong enough but before opponents can contest the center. Holding the center for three or four rounds adds enormous VP.

walkthrough.title: Diplomatic VP Strategy
walkthrough.text: The diplomatic approach is subtle but effective. Each ambassador tile is worth one victory point at game end, and you can accumulate several. Propose diplomacy to every neighbor early. Use the Diplomacy action to collect three or four ambassador tiles over the course of the game. This gives you free VP while keeping your borders safe. Meanwhile, invest in economy and technology. The diplomatic player avoids the costs of war while scoring guaranteed points. The main risk is that a militant opponent might not accept your proposals, but even then, the threat of the Traitor Card deters many would-be aggressors.

walkthrough.title: Technology Rush Strategy
walkthrough.text: Going deep in one technology color unlocks massive discounts that let you research four or five techs in that color for almost nothing by the end of the game. The tech rush strategy picks one color and commits. Military tech gives you weapons and ship components. Grid tech gives you economy and production bonuses. Nano tech gives you special abilities and victory points. Each tech you place on your faction board may also advance your VP track. A player with six or seven technologies in one color can score five to eight VP from the track alone, while having access to the most powerful abilities in that color.

walkthrough.title: Adaptive Play and Reading the Table
walkthrough.text: The best Eclipse players do not commit to a single strategy from the start. They read the table and adapt. If your neighbors are peaceful, lean into economy and diplomacy. If your neighbor is building Dreadnoughts, you need defensive tech and starbases. If the GCDS path is uncontested, pivot to a center rush. Watch what technologies your opponents research, because that tells you their plans. If someone grabs Plasma Missiles, they are coming for someone. If someone is researching Grid tech, they are building an economic engine. Adapt your ship designs to counter the biggest threat at the table.

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Tempo and Timing
walkthrough.text: Rounds seven through nine are where games are won and lost. The most common late-game mistake is continuing to build infrastructure when you should be scoring. Every action in the final three rounds should have a clear VP payoff. If you have a fleet ready, strike in round seven to claim high-value sectors and earn reputation tiles. If you are diplomatic, ensure all your ambassadors are in place. If you are economic, make sure every planet is colonized and producing. The final round is your last chance to reposition ships to defend your VP-rich sectors. Players who execute a clean endgame sprint consistently win close games.

walkthrough.title: Counter-Strategies and Meta Awareness
walkthrough.text: Understanding what beats what is the final layer of Eclipse mastery. Economic players lose to aggressive military players who take their sectors. Military players lose to defensive players with starbases and strong alliances who bleed them dry. Diplomatic players lose when their allies betray them at the critical moment. Tech rush players lose if opponents deny them key technologies by purchasing them first. The counter to any strategy is awareness. If you see someone executing a specific plan, take the action that disrupts it. Buy the tech they need. Fortify the sector they want. Propose diplomacy with their target. The meta-game of reading and countering opponents is what elevates Eclipse from great to masterpiece.

entries.definition: Action that lets you draw hex tiles from one of the three ring stacks and place one adjacent to a sector you control via wormhole connections. You may claim the new sector with an influence disc or discard the tile.

Action that gives you two influence moves. Use these to place influence discs on sectors to claim them, remove discs from sectors to abandon them, or place population cubes on planet slots to colonize them.
Action that lets you buy one technology tile from the supply board by paying its Science cost, minus cumulative discounts from your existing tech in that color row.
Action that gives you two upgrade moves to swap components on your ship blueprints. Changes apply to all future ships built, not to existing ships on the map.
Action that lets you spend Materials to construct up to two ships or starbases, placed in sectors you control. Ship cost is determined by the blueprint tile.
Action that gives you three move sub-actions. Each sub-action moves one ship up to its drive value in sectors through wormhole connections. You can move the same ship multiple times.
Action that lets you propose an alliance with another player. If both agree, exchange ambassador tiles worth 1 VP each. Breaking an alliance gives you the Traitor Card worth -2 VP.
Instead of taking an action, you pass for the rest of the round. The first player to pass gets the best turn order position for the next round. You cannot take any more actions after passing.

A disc from your faction board used to claim sectors on the map and to mark actions on the action track. Removing discs from the faction board uncovers higher production values but also increases upkeep costs.

A cost in Money paid at the end of each round based on the total number of influence discs you have deployed on the map and action track. The upkeep curve increases steeply with more discs. If you cannot pay, you must remove discs from the map.
When a player cannot pay their upkeep cost in Money during the Upkeep Phase. The player must remove influence discs from the map, abandoning sectors, until their upkeep drops to an affordable level.
One of the three resources. Used to pay upkeep costs during the Upkeep Phase. Produced by colonized orange planets and uncovered Money track values on your faction board.
One of the three resources. Used to pay research costs for technology tiles. Produced by colonized pink planets and uncovered Science track values on your faction board.
One of the three resources. Used to pay build costs for ships and starbases. Produced by colonized brown planets and uncovered Materials track values on your faction board.

Three tracks on your faction board (Money, Science, Materials) covered by influence discs. As discs are removed to claim sectors or take actions, higher production values are uncovered. The highest uncovered value on each track determines your production.

A small cube placed on planet slots within sectors you control. Population cubes represent colonists and enable resource production from that planet. Orange planets produce Money, pink produce Science, brown produce Materials.
Your personal player board containing resource tracks, technology placement rows, influence disc storage, and upkeep information. The center of your economic management.

The smallest and cheapest ship type. Fast with good initiative, typically fires first in combat. Low hull means it is destroyed by a single hit. Best used for scouting, screening, and early aggression.
A mid-range ship type balancing cost, firepower, and durability. The workhorse of most fleets. Has more component slots than an Interceptor and is more affordable than a Dreadnought.
The largest and most expensive ship type. Multiple weapon and hull slots make it a devastating combatant. Slower initiative but can absorb many hits. The backbone of a military strategy.

An immobile defensive structure that cannot move but can participate in combat in its sector. Typically loaded with shields and hull for maximum durability. Does not require a drive component. Excellent for holding key sectors.

A tile showing the component layout for one ship type. Each blueprint has slots for weapons, drives, computers, shields, hulls, and energy sources. When you upgrade a blueprint, all future ships of that type are built with the new design.

A ship stat that determines firing order in combat. Ships with the lowest initiative value fire first. Interceptors typically have the best initiative. Initiative is determined by the components installed on the ship blueprint.

A ship component that adds a bonus to all attack die rolls for that ship. A +2 computer means a roll of 4 becomes 6, which hits. Higher computer values dramatically improve hit rates. Countered by enemy shields.

A ship component that subtracts from enemy attack die rolls against that ship. A -1 shield makes the attacker's roll of 6 count as 5, which misses. Shields counter computers and make ships harder to hit.
A ship component that determines how many hits a ship can absorb before being destroyed. Each hull point absorbs one hit. A ship with hull value 2 survives two hits but is destroyed on the third.

A ship component that determines how many sectors a ship can move per move sub-action. Higher drive values allow greater map mobility. Also contributes to initiative value. Starbases do not require or use drives.

A ship component that provides energy to power all other components. Every weapon, drive, computer, shield, and hull consumes energy. Total energy must be equal to or greater than total consumption, or the blueprint is illegal.

A ship component that provides attack dice in combat. Each weapon typically grants one or more dice. Weapon types include Ion Cannon, Plasma Missile, Antimatter Cannon, and others, each with different damage, energy costs, and hit modifiers.
The basic starting weapon component. Provides one attack die. Low energy cost and moderate effectiveness. Most ship blueprints start with Ion Cannons before being upgraded to better weapons.
An advanced weapon component researched from the Military tech track. Deals additional damage and has favorable hit characteristics. A popular upgrade over Ion Cannons for mid-game combat.

One of the most powerful weapon components in the game. High damage output but significant energy cost. Research cost is steep but devastating when installed on Dreadnoughts with sufficient energy sources.

A Military technology that lets you eliminate enemy population cubes from planets after winning combat in a sector. Allows you to depopulate enemy sectors, denying them resource production even if they reclaim the sector.

One of the three technology colors. Military techs provide ship components (weapons, computers, hull upgrades) and combat-related abilities. Cumulative research discounts apply within the Military row.
One of the three technology colors. Grid techs provide economic bonuses, production improvements, energy sources, and drive upgrades. Rewards players focusing on infrastructure and expansion.
One of the three technology colors. Nano techs provide special abilities, hull upgrades, shield components, and unique powers. Often grants direct victory points or powerful defensive capabilities.

The hex tiles closest to the Galactic Center, marked with Roman numeral I. These sectors have the richest planets and highest VP values but are contested and close to the dangerous GCDS. High-risk, high-reward territory.
The hex tiles forming the second ring from the Galactic Center, marked with Roman numeral II. Moderate resources and VP values. A common battleground where player territories meet.
The hex tiles farthest from the Galactic Center, marked with Roman numeral III. Lower VP values and fewer planets but safer from opponents in the early game. Most players start exploring here.

Connection points on hex tile edges that allow ships to travel between adjacent sectors. Tiles must be placed so that wormhole connections match. Ships can only move between sectors connected by wormholes.

A powerful Ancient NPC ship guarding the center hex tile. Worth 4 VP to the player who defeats it and controls the center. Has very high combat stats and requires a strong, purpose-built fleet to defeat.

NPC ships found on some hex tiles when explored. They guard the sector and must be defeated in combat before the sector can be claimed. Ancients do not move or take actions but fight back with their printed combat stats. Defeating Ancients often reveals discovery tiles.

A tile placed face-down on explored sectors that have a discovery symbol. Flipped and claimed when the sector is colonized. Benefits include free resources, technologies, ship components, or VP. Can be kept for its benefit or flipped for an instant 2 VP.

A small circular token with a hidden VP value of 1 to 4. Drawn from the bag after winning combat, one per enemy ship type destroyed. The winner peeks at the drawn tiles, keeps the best one, and returns the rest. A source of hidden VP that can swing the final score.

A tile exchanged between two players when they form a diplomatic alliance. Each ambassador tile is worth 1 VP at game end. If the alliance is broken, the ambassador is returned and the betrayer takes the Traitor Card.

A penalty card worth -2 VP given to any player who attacks a player they have a diplomatic agreement with. Only one Traitor Card exists. If a second player betrays an ally, the card transfers to them. A strong deterrent against breaking diplomatic pacts.
Victory points printed on each hex tile. Sectors in the inner ring are worth more VP than those in the outer ring. You score VP only for sectors you control (have an influence disc on) at game end.

Victory points earned by advancing along the technology tracks on your faction board. As you research more technologies in a color, you may unlock VP milestones. Deep specialization can yield 5-8 VP from a single track.

A row on the game board or faction board where influence discs are placed each time you take an action. The total number of discs on the action track plus the map determines your upkeep cost. Discs are returned to the faction board during the Cleanup step of Upkeep.

A track showing the order in which players take their turns during the Action Phase. Updated at the end of each round based on the order in which players passed. First to pass gets the first position.
A numbered track from 1 to 9 showing the current round. The round marker advances one space after each Upkeep Phase. The game ends after the marker reaches 9.

A mechanism in some factions or through technology that allows you to colonize advanced planet types. Colony ships enable you to place population cubes on orbital or advanced planet slots that are otherwise inaccessible.
A planet type that produces Money when colonized with a population cube. Orange planets are critical for funding upkeep costs and maintaining economic stability.
A planet type that produces Science when colonized with a population cube. Pink planets fuel technology research, which is essential for upgrading ship blueprints and gaining special abilities.
A planet type that produces Materials when colonized with a population cube. Brown planets provide the resources needed to build ships and starbases.

After each combat round, surviving ships may retreat to an adjacent sector the player controls. Retreated ships are flipped face-down and cannot participate in combat for the rest of the round. Retreating can save valuable ships from destruction.

A single cycle of combat within a battle. Each combat round involves all surviving ships firing in initiative order, then players deciding whether to retreat. Combat continues in rounds until only one player remains or all ships have retreated.
